challenge_summary: Governance reconciliation found two blocking gaps: the live workflow can open the backend runner firewall after the approved exception expires, and an inherited allowed-locations assignment is present in canonical JSON but not reconciled into allowed_locations or the Markdown constraints. A non-blocking tag-contract drift remains between canonical tags_required and the exact-context/runtime tag subset.
compact_for_parent: Pass 1 (governance-reconciliation) | HIGH | 2 must_fix, 1 should_fix | Key: Workflow can open runner firewall access after exception expiry; Allowed-location assignment is present but not reconciled
residual_risks: Live deployment, OIDC token exchange, Azure RBAC propagation, and actual data-plane access were not exercised during this review and remain unproven.
The approved public-network exception is treated as a bounded non-production sandbox decision; no defect is recorded for the exception itself while it remains within scope and unexpired.
Policy parameter values that the canonical JSON marks unavailable or unresolved require live refresh or explicit human verification before being treated as deployment proof.

findings.claim: Workflow can open runner firewall access after exception expiry
findings.evidence: The canonical governance JSON records security exception vnext-qualification-backend-runner-ip for control public-network-access with requested_at 2026-07-15T12:50:34Z, expires_at 2026-07-16T12:50:34Z, and scope.maximum_lifetime_hours 24. The Markdown Approved Security Exception repeats the same expiry and describes one ephemeral runner /32. The live workflow validates protected inputs by running tools/scripts/validate-vnext-qualification-context.mjs before adding firewall rules, but that validator only checks subscription, resource names, location, backend naming, workspace ID, and APEX_QUALIFICATION_TAGS_JSON. It does not inspect security_exceptions or expires_at. The workflow then runs az storage account network-rule add in both preview and apply jobs. The static security exception library can detect expired exceptions when validate-iac-security-baseline scans source, but this runtime workflow path does not call that validator before opening the firewall.
findings.impact: A manual workflow dispatch after 2026-07-16T12:50:34Z could still add a GitHub-hosted runner /32 to the backend storage firewall, exceeding the approved sandbox exception and authorizing public endpoint access outside the recorded governance window.
findings.artifact_section: Approved Security Exception; vNext Live Qualification workflow

suggested_fix.proposed_edit: Before each `Open temporary firewall rule` step, call a local validator that reads `agent-output/vnext-qualification/04-governance-constraints.json`, finds exactly one `security_exceptions[]` entry named `vnext-qualification-backend-runner-ip`, verifies `control == public-network-access`, `scope.environment == vnext-qualification`, `scope.workload == terraform-backend`, `scope.maximum_lifetime_hours <= 24`, and `expires_at > now`, then fails closed before `az storage account network-rule add` when the exception is absent, expired, or out of scope.

verification_anchors: .github/workflows/vnext-live-qualification.yml preview Validate protected inputs before Open temporary firewall rule
.github/workflows/vnext-live-qualification.yml apply Validate protected inputs before Open temporary firewall rule
tools/scripts/_lib/security-exceptions.mjs securityExceptionIssues expiry check exists only for callers that invoke it
tools/scripts/validate-iac-security-baseline.mjs calls securityExceptionIssues during static IaC scan

findings.claim: Allowed-location assignment is present but not reconciled
findings.evidence: The canonical JSON assignment_inventory includes management-group inherited assignments named `JV - Allowed Locations` with policyDefinitionId ending e56962a6-4747-49cd-b67b-bf8b01975c4c and `Resource Group and Resource locations should match`, but the same JSON has allowed_locations as an empty array and no location_constraints object. The Markdown artifact states `No network-specific policies discovered` and the downstream Bicep/Terraform plans state `No allowed-location policy was discovered. The selected region remains swedencentral for all resources.` This creates a contradiction between the inventory of inherited assignments and the generated constraints consumed by implementation.
findings.impact: If the inherited allowed-locations assignment has a deny effect or unextracted parameters that exclude swedencentral, Step 4/5 can plan and deploy resources into a region that Azure Policy rejects. If swedencentral is allowed, the artifact still lacks the evidence needed to prove that location reconciliation was completed.
findings.artifact_section: Discovery Source; Network Policies; Governance Contract

suggested_fix.proposed_edit: Reconcile the inherited `JV - Allowed Locations` assignment from `assignment_inventory`: extract its effect and parameters into `allowed_locations` / `location_constraints`, or mark it as an unresolved blocker with an explicit Step 4 hold. Update the Markdown location statement so it no longer says no allowed-location policy was discovered unless the assignment is verified non-applicable.

verification_anchors: agent-output/vnext-qualification/04-governance-constraints.json assignment_inventory entries `JV - Allowed Locations` and `Resource Group and Resource locations should match`
agent-output/vnext-qualification/04-governance-constraints.json allowed_locations is [] and location_constraints is absent
agent-output/vnext-qualification/04-governance-constraints.md Network Policies says no network-specific policies discovered
infra/bicep/vnext-qualification/.azure/plan.md Governance Contract says no allowed-location policy was discovered and selected region remains swedencentral
infra/terraform/vnext-qualification/.azure/plan.md Governance Contract says no allowed-location policy was discovered and selected region remains swedencentral

findings.claim: Runtime tag validator omits canonical SecurityControl and unresolved tags
findings.evidence: The canonical JSON `tags_required` array includes `SecurityControl`, nine deny-required lowercase tags from `JV-Enforce Resource Group Tags v3`, and two unresolved tag entries from inherited tag policies. The Markdown Required Tags section lists `SecurityControl`, `tech-contact`, `technical-contact`, and unresolved tag rows. However, tools/scripts/validate-vnext-qualification-context.mjs defines EXPECTED_TAGS as exactly ten keys: the nine lowercase contract values plus `tech-contact`; it omits `SecurityControl` and does not force an explicit unresolved-tag disposition. The Bicep and Terraform plan Governance Contract sections also describe the resource groups as using nine lowercase deny-required tags plus `tech-contact`.
findings.impact: Qualification resources may be created with a narrower tag set than the canonical governance extraction records. Modify policies may then mutate tags outside the exact-context evidence, or a later refresh that resolves the inherited tag keys could turn the currently unresolved entries into deployment blockers without a corresponding runtime contract update.
findings.artifact_section: Required Tags; Governance Contract; exact-context validator

suggested_fix.proposed_edit: Derive the expected qualification tag keys from `agent-output/vnext-qualification/04-governance-constraints.json tags_required`, require `SecurityControl` when it remains in the canonical contract, and require an explicit allow/skip record for unresolved inherited tag entries instead of silently narrowing the runtime tag contract to ten hardcoded keys.

agent-output/vnext-qualification/04-governance-constraints.md Required Tags lists SecurityControl, technical-contact, tech-contact, and unresolved rows
tools/scripts/validate-vnext-qualification-context.mjs EXPECTED_TAGS contains ten hardcoded keys and checks APEX_QUALIFICATION_TAGS_JSON against that object
